reasoning: All four correctly identify the core flaws (post hoc causation, the confounded 2020 year, the undecomposed revenue/employee ratio, survivorship bias in the competitor comparison, false-dichotomy remedy) and reach the same sound verdict, so discrimination comes from calibration, bias breadth, and second-order depth. C wins decisively: it is the only response that states an explicit calibrated probability ("well under 30%" that remote is the primary cause), names the widest set of biases (post hoc, selection/survivorship, resulting, regression to the mean), and adds genuinely advanced moves, a pre-stated falsifier and a premortem on the mandate. A is a close second with the strongest unique contribution, a red-team scenario where a five-day mandate backfires and worsens the very metric cited, plus the cleanest structure, but it offers no numeric confidence. D earns third on a sharp suggestion to use real within-firm output metrics rather than the financial ratio and a stated low confidence, edging out B, which is thorough and well-organized but the most conventional, with no explicit calibration and fewer distinct biases named.
